text: I am sharing a case of complicated diverticulitis. This gentleman had had numerous attacks over a period of about fifteen years and surprisingly was asymptomatic at this point but had an impassable what was thought to be a stricture on attempted colonoscopy and couldn't really visualize and so he presents for surgery.
The surgeon carefully dissects and separates the small bowel adhered to the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon observes a potential fistula adherence as the tissue is mobilized and elects to over-sew the area using interrupted trio silk sutures.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, likely focusing on separating the sigmoid colon from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon enters the retroperitoneal plane, which remains intact despite severe diverticular disease. Ureteral catheters are visible, and the surgeon checks for ICG fluorescence to ensure ureter safety without extensive dissection.
The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle, carefully dissecting it free from surrounding tissue. The blood vessel is then divided using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection of the vasculature, identifying the superior hemorrhoidal vein and preparing to transect it.

The surgeon attempts medial dissection towards the lateral side but encounters adherence of the sigmoid colon to the pelvic sidewall, preventing further progress. The surgeon then transitions to initiating lateral dissection.

And this part of the case, I think highlights, you know, the principles of the surgery for any surgery, which is go where easy or work where easy. Right now, this just looks easy. And I know that I'm going to need to do a full lateral mobilization, including splenic flexure. So I'm just working on this right now and leaving the hard stuff for a little bit later.

The surgeon continues dissection towards the splenic flexure using scissors, maintaining the current port setup without repositioning. As the dissection progresses higher, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer to securely divide the lateral attachments.

The surgeon continues lateral mobilization, joining into the previously dissected medial plane. A sponge is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses ICG imaging to identify the ureter, allowing for confident dissection of more challenging areas before moving back up.

The surgeon continues lateral mobilization, focusing on separating the sigmoid colon from the bladder. The tissue shows chronic adherence, requiring careful dissection to free the sigmoid without a fistula present.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to check for any green fluorescence indicating bladder injury during dissection. The suction device is employed to manage the surgical field, while granulation tissue is observed, suggesting potential fistula formation. The ICG confirms no bladder injury, and the surgeon navigates through the colon wall, noting granulation tissue.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, followed by a limited rectal mobilization to decrease tension in the anastomosis. The mesentery is lifted off the kidney, and the splenic flexure is released from a lateral approach.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect around the spleen, pulling the colon to keep it clear of the surgical field. A capsular tear occurs, causing bleeding, which is managed using bipolar cautery. The surgeon continues the lateral approach, elevating the descending colon, mesentery, and splenic flexure from the kidney while searching for the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon adjusts the table to reduce the Trendelenburg position, improving the view without additional ports or redocking. The dissection proceeds into the gastrocolic ligament, entering the lesser sac. The transverse colon mesentery is carefully dissected off the inferior border of the pancreas, continuing the release of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon clears the distal mesentery using a vessel sealer after successfully mobilizing the entire splenic flexure. The decision is made to extract the specimen through a Pfannenstiel incision rather than transrectally, ensuring the integrity of the oncologic operation.

The surgeon marks the proximal margin of the colon, noting the woody appearance of the mesentery due to disease. The surgeon adjusts the margin a few centimeters proximal to ensure complete removal of diseased tissue, aiming to reduce the risk of recurrence.
The surgeon divides the distal section of the colon, followed by the proximal section, using surgical instruments to separate these segments.
The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis by first sewing the staple lines together, followed by creating a proctotomy and colotomy. A two-layer suture technique is used to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon places the final stitches in the anastomosis site, then cleans the surgical area. The pelvis is flooded with fluid to perform a leak test, showing no bubbles, indicating a successful procedure.

I believe they were discharged on the morning of post-operative day two. Really never had any pain, more stoicism for the patient than any credit by me. Like I said, I extracted the specimen through a fan and steel incision. I hope you enjoyed.
